coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sequential ‘ON-OFF-ON’ fluorescence probe for the sensitive detection of Fe3+ and l-alanine/l-histidine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Emami, Elham
Mousazadeh, Mohammad H.
coredata.dc:description: Herein, the innovative and highly effective nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) fluorescent probes were conveniently fabricated using spirulina algae and l-arginine as the raw materials by using a one-pot hydrothermal treatment. The as-prepared N-CDs exhibited great stability, exceptional optical performance, superior biocompatibility as well as low cytotoxicity. The fluorescence of N-CDs had the most vigorous green light emission around 530 nm upon excitation at 450 nm with a 32 % quantum yield, which can be employed as a ‘turn-off’ response for Fe3+ detecting. The limit of detection for Fe3+ by the N-CDs is 14.8 nM with a linear response in the range of 0.05–1 µM. Therefore, the static quenching mechanism for the fluorescence of N-CDs for detection was proposed, which was due to the Fe3+could easily be combined with functional groups on the surface of N-CDs and the induced aggregation. The N-CDs nanosensor can serve as a reversible fluorescence quenching nanoprobe for Fe3+. As confirmed by adding amino acids containing l-histidine and l-alanine to the complex of N-CDs-Fe3+ that caused a ‘turn-on’ fluorescence response. Compared to other nanoprobes found in the literature, N-CDs is capable of detecting the l-alanine or l-histidine with a detection limit as low as 52.8 nM and 69 nM with a good linear relationship in the range of 0.5–50 µM, respectively. In addition, the newly developed fluorescence nanosensor was successfully used to determine Fe3+ in tap water and mineral water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: l-alanine
l-Histidine
Probe
Fluorescence
Static quenching
Fe3+ ions

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of amino acid using N-CDs-Fe3+
Biocompatibility and cellular imaging capacity of N-CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are genuinely thankful to the Amirkabir University of Technology (Tehran, Iran), and the Iranian National Science Foundation (INSF-97016250) for the financial support of this research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM graph of the obtained N-CDs, (b) HR-TEM image of the N-CDs, (c) dynamic light scattering spectra of the prepared N-CDs, (d) powder X-ray diffraction spectra of N-CDs, (e) FT-IR spectrum of the prepared N-CDs.
(a) XPS spectrum of N-CDs, the corresponding deconvolution spectra of (b) C 1s peak, (c) N 1s peak, and (d) O 1s peak.
(a) UV–visible absorption and emission spectra of the prepared N-CDs (λex = 450 nm), (b) Fluorescence intensity spectra of the N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths (400–490 nm).

Fluorescence emission spectra of the obtained N-CDs in aqueous solution under (a) Fluorescence continuous excitation at 450 nm for 120 min using a fluorescence spectrometer equipped with a xenon flash lamp, (b) various concentrations of NaCl (0–2.5 M), (c) ambient conditions over time (1, 3, 7,14 and 30 days), (d) different pH.

Competitive selectivity of the prepared N-CDs for Fe3+ over other metal ions under pH = 7 condition at 450 nm excitation (the red bars represent the addition of metals ions (2.5 mg L-1) o the N-CDs solution and the green bars represent the subsequent addition of Fe3+ (2.5 mg L-1) to the N-CDs solution.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs in the presence of various concentrations of Fe3+ (0–50 μM), (b) Stern-Volmer plot for fluorescence intensity quenching of N-CDs by Fe3+ in the range of (0–50 μM).
The fluorescence intensity behavior of N-CDs by adding Fe3+ and EDTA alternately.
The effect of different amino acids (200 μM) in (a) the presence of prepared N-CDs with Fe3+ ions (50 μM) and in (b) the absence of Fe3+ ions.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs in the presence of Fe3+ with different concentrations of l-alanine (0–100 µM), (b) Stern-Volmer plot for fluorescence intensity returning of N-CDs with Fe3+ by l-alanine concentration in the range of (0–100 μM).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of obtained N-CDs in the presence of Fe3+ ion with various concentrations of l-histidine (0–100 µM), (b) Stern-Volmer plot for fluorescence recovering of N-CDs with Fe3+ by l-histidine concentration in the range of (0–100 μM).
(a) Fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs before and after the addition of Fe3+, (b) Cyclic voltammetry of the prepared N-CDs.

(a) The viability of Hela cells with various concentrations of the obtained N-CDs (0–1000 μg mL−1). (b) Fluorescence microscopy image of Hela cells (a-d) and their corresponding bright filed (á-d́) ((a) the cells were incubated with N-CDs (50 μg mL−1), (b) cells containing N-CDs incubated with various concentrations of Fe3+ (1 μM), (c) 25 μM, (d) 50 μM, respectively).
Schematic illustration of the preparation and versatile applications of N-CDs.
(a) quenching mechanism of N-CDs and (b) energy level simulation.
Comparison of various fluorescent CDs-based sensors to detect Fe3+.
Comparison of different methods to detect amino acids l-histidine and l-alanine.

row.entry: Chiral carbazole with urea-carboxylic acid moiety (CCS)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of Fe3+ in the tap and mineral water samples.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sequential ‘ON-OFF-ON’ fluorescence probe for the sensitive detection of Fe3+ and l-alanine/l-histidine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Amirkabir University of Technology, 424 Hafez Avenue, Tehran P.O Box: 15875-4413, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Amirkabir University of Technology, 424 Hafez Avenue, Tehran P.O Box: 15875-4413, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, the innovative and highly effective nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) fluorescent probes were conveniently fabricated using spirulina algae and l-arginine as the raw materials by using a one-pot hydrothermal treatment. The as-prepared N-CDs exhibited great stability, exceptional optical performance, superior biocompatibility as well as low cytotoxicity. The fluorescence of N-CDs had the most vigorous green light emission around 530 nm upon excitation at 450 nm with a 32 % quantum yield, which can be employed as a ‘turn-off’ response for Fe3+ detecting. The limit of detection for Fe3+ by the N-CDs is 14.8 nM with a linear response in the range of 0.05–1 µM. Therefore, the static quenching mechanism for the fluorescence of N-CDs for detection was proposed, which was due to the Fe3+could easily be combined with functional groups on the surface of N-CDs and the induced aggregation. The N-CDs nanosensor can serve as a reversible fluorescence quenching nanoprobe for Fe3+. As confirmed by adding amino acids containing l-histidine and l-alanine to the complex of N-CDs-Fe3+ that caused a ‘turn-on’ fluorescence response. Compared to other nanoprobes found in the literature, N-CDs is capable of detecting the l-alanine or l-histidine with a detection limit as low as 52.8 nM and 69 nM with a good linear relationship in the range of 0.5–50 µM, respectively. In addition, the newly developed fluorescence nanosensor was successfully used to determine Fe3+ in tap water and mineral water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the development of fluorescent probes capable of detecting metallic transition ions as well as amino acids has become extremely important for biological and environmental applications. Fe3+ as the vital transitional elements for the necessary activities of life play a critical role in a number of pathological and physiological processes, like the transport of oxygen, cellular metabolism, enzyme, and catalysis [1–3]. Excessive consumption of Fe3+ can disturb cellar metabolism and lead to a series of illnesses [4,5]. Thus, the development of a cost-effective and straightforward approach to the monitoring of Fe3+ is needed. Up to now, much effort has been devoted to detecting Fe3+; indeed, these detection strategies were cumbersome for preparation, detection of analytes, unsatisfying detection limit and costly instrumentation [6]. In fact, many types of fluorescent probes have been constructed that include new metal/covalent organic frames, traditional fluorescent organic molecules, carbon dots, and chemically modified CDs/ZnS quantum dots [7]. Recently years, fluorescent sensors attracted a great deal of attention due to their high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, good water solubility, low cytotoxicity, environmental friendliness, and bioimaging [8]. CDs originated from various carbon sources and surface functionalization attempts of molecules have been demonstrated to be of great success for the purpose of fluorescent imaging of biological cells and bio-sensing [9–11]. Therefore, this property of CDs has made feasible their use in biological applications such as in vivo and in vitro bioimaging, gene delivery, nanomedicine, and drug and as carriers that can penetrate the blood–brain barrier [12,13]. To date, a number of methods using CDs for sensing Fe3+ have been reported. Sun and co-workers [14] fabricated these first-ever carbon-based nanoparticles for quantitative detection of Fe3+ in water solution through the internal filtration effects that cause the quenching fluorescence. Jiang and co-workers [15] proposed a fluorescent “turn-off” probe-based on N-CDs for the sensitive determination of Fe3+ in aqueous samples.

As amino acids are playing a vital role in physiological processes [16], identifying traces of amino acids is required for the diagnosis of the disease, dietary analysis, and biomedical research. Among these, histidine is critical for tissue growth and for transmitting metal components [17]. Histidine deficiency in plasma can lead to nutritional problems in patients suffering from chronic renal disease [18]. Besides, alanine plays a critical role in the glucose-alanine cycle between tissues and the liver. This cycle plays an important role and permits pyruvate and glutamate to be removed from the muscles and be safely transported to the liver [19]. Therefore, the development of efficient detection strategies for the detection and quantification of alanine and histidine has generated considerable interest. Detection of amino acids is typically carried out using liquid chromatography [20,21], electrochemical analysis, capillary electrophoresis [22], and capillary electrochromatography [23,24]. Nevertheless, these procedures are lengthy and cumbersome, requiring relatively costly equipment and extra pretreatment to enhance selectivity and sensitivity. Thus, the development of efficient probes for the detection of specific amino acids is still in great demand, in particular sensors based on the metal complex.

The development of fluorescent sensors for efficient and straightforward visual sensing of unique amino acids or ions with elevated sensitivity and selectivity has become increasingly important. Fast and inexpensive detection of specific amino acids and metal ions can be performed through sequential color change [25–29]. Kim and co-workers [30] reported a chemosensor for the sequential sensing of Cu2+ and amino acids, including histidine and cysteine in the aqueous solution. Pundi and his colleague [31] proposed a sensor for sequential ‘on–off-on’ based on carbazole for detecting Fe3+ and amino acids, including tryptophan and histidine. Li and co-workers [32] suggested the ‘turn on’ probe for sensing α-amino acids in the presence of β- and γ-amino acids. The fluorescence detection of amino acids and biothiols using appropriate probes attracted a great deal of attention due to their operational simplicity, quick response, cost-effectiveness, and excellent sensitivity [33,34]. Unknown analytes have traditionally been found in an aqueous solution which their analysis in a solvent having a high-water inclusion is best suited for the practical analysis.

In this current study, our goal is to design an ‘ON-OFF-ON’ fluorescent sensor that can quickly and selectively detect Fe3+ and amino acids containing l-histidine and l-alanine through N-CDs. The N-CDs have been synthesized via a facile bottom-up hydrothermal approach by using spirulina algae and amino acid including l-arginine, as carbon and nitrogen precursors. The preceding feature led to the high quantum yield of the N-CDs as well as to the functionalization of the surface N-CDs and, as a result, to the improvement of the binding selectivity. To the best of our knowledge, the determination of the amino acids comprising l-alanine and l-histidine simultaneously in the presence of Fe3+ ion with N-CDs as a high selectivity nanosensor has not been reported in the literature so far. Most intriguingly, the N-CDs could efficiently act as a ‘turn-off ‘probe for Fe3+ sensing via a static quenching process. Meanwhile, their fluorescence could be immediately restored in the presence of l-histidine and l-alanine amino acids into the complex of N-CDs-Fe3+. Besides, we extend that a quantitative sensing Fe3+ in the real sample. The effects of the preliminary biological assessment indicate that these N-CDs possess low cytotoxicity, which permitted their use in practical application. Based on these results, the prepared N-CDs could be exploited for biosensing and bioimaging (Scheme 1).

Spirulina algae powder has been obtained from a local market. All the metals as As(Cl)3, Pb(NO3)2, Cu(NO3)2, Hg(NO3)2, Rh(NO3)3, Pt(NO3)4, Cr(NO3)3, Ag(NO3), Fe(NO3)3, Zn(NO3)2, Ca(NO3)2, Ni(NO3), Cd(NO3)2, SeO2, Mn(NO3)2, (NH₄)₆MO₇O₂₄, Co(NO3)2, Al(NO3)3, Pd(NO3)2, NaCl Purity of (>98 %) were obtained from Merck. l-lysine, l-tryptophan, l-arginine, l-histidine, l-cysteine, l-glycine, l-glutamine, l-alanine, l-valine, and l-tyrosine were supplied from Merck. The dialysis membranes were purchased from co-chemicals (Iran). All solutions were prepared using doubly distilled water (DW) in experimental procedures. All the reagents were used without additional treatment.

To investigate the size distribution and the morphology of N-CDs, a transmission electron microscope (TEM) images (FEI TEC9G 20, USA, operating at a voltage of 200 kv) was used. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was obtained using a Bruker (alpha) spectrometer (Karlsruhe, Germany). Fluorescence lifetime was taken by using an Edinburgh FLS920 (UK) with a Xenon lamp. Photoluminescence (PL) property measurements were performed using a Spectro fluorophotometer (Perkin Elmer LS55, USA), Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption was carried out by (Perkin Elmer Lambda 45, USA). To examine the chemical composition of the N-CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed (BESTEC, Germany) using a monochromatic Al- Kα X-ray source (hv = 1486.6 eV) and a vacuum system of 10-10 mbar. X-ray diffraction (XRD) profiles of the N-CDs were analyzed by Philips company using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 A°). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and Zeta potential of the N-CDs were measured by an instrument (Cordouan Tech (WALLIS), France). Raman Scattering spectra were obtained on (Teksan, Tehran, Iran) spectrometer equipped with a laser source at 532 nm. Fluorescence images studies were done on a CKX41 reverse fluorescence microscope (Olympus, Japan), and cytotoxicity assays were carried out by using an MTT assay.

The N-CDs were successfully prepared by a facile bottom-up hydrothermal carbonization technique. In brief, 10 mg of algae and 30 mg of l-arginine powder were dissolved in 30 mL ultrapure water. Then, the solution was put into a poly-(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon) autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 10 h. Next, the bright yellow solution has cooled naturally at ambient temperature. Subsequently, the resulting products were centrifuged at 14000 rpm for 20 min, and the supernatants were dialyzed to remove any unreacted materials and impurities by (molecular weight = 3.5 K Da) for overnight to obtain pure N-CDs solution [35]. Finally, the solution was held at 4 °C for further usage, and the solutions were stable for an extended period without precipitation.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the synthesized N-CDs were determined by a common method according to previous reports [35] using Rhodamine 6G in ethanol (QY = 95 %) as a reference. The fluorescence QY value of N-CDs was calculated as follows:
Q
Y
t
= Q
Y r
×
I t
I r
×
A r
A t
×
η t 2
η r 2
where QY is the quantum yield and the subscript t and r refer to the testing sample and the standards reference, respectively. Also, A is the optical density, I is the integrated emission intensity, and ŋ is the refractive index of the solvent. To get more reliable results, the absorption of the N-CDs was adjusted to less than 0.06 to prevent the re-absorption effect.

To determine the method of recovery, tap water and mineral water were employed to show the practicality of the N-CDs probe for detection in aqueous matrices. The tap water samples have been collected from our laboratory and the mineral water samples have been taken from a local market. Prior to the trial, the real water samples have primitively been filtered with a 0.45 µm pore size membrane to remove the solid impurities and then stored at 4 °C until analysis. For the determination of Fe3+, These real water samples have been spiked with metal Fe3+ ions of desired concentrations. Finally, the prepared N-CDs solution is added to the spiked real samples, shaken well, and the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded (λex = 450 nm, λem = 530 nm).

To evaluate the cytotoxicity of N-CDs, the biosafety assay was implemented through an MTT assay. Typically, Human cervical carcinoma (Hela) cells were grown into 96-well culture plates in Dulbecco’s minimum essential media (DMEM) complemented 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1 % glutamine and incubation at 37 °C for 24 h with 5 % CO2 atmosphere. Then, the culture medium was replaced with 100 µL fresh DMEM containing N-CD with varying concentrations (0–1000 µg mL−1) and incubated overnight at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere containing 5 % CO2. Subsequently, the MTT reagent was added to each cell and the culture medium were treated for further another 4 h. Finally, the cell viability was determined by using (Tecan, Sunrise, Austria).

Hela cells were seeded in a cover-glass-bottom dish in a DMEM medium containing 10 % FBS overnight at 37 °C under a humidified atmosphere containing 5 % CO2. In general, the stocking medium of N-CDs (0.5 µg mL−1) were added to Hela cells and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h. After incubation, the cells were washed with phosphate-buffered solution (PBS) buffer three times to eliminate the extracellular free N-CDs, then the cells were retained in PBS for cellular imaging by using a microscopic image. In order to assess Fe3+ ion uptake, the Hela cells were first treated with N-CDs for 2 h, followed by the incubation of various concentrations of Fe3+ion (1, 25, 50 µg mL−1) for 20 min at 37 °C, and the fluorescence images of cells were obtained.

The CDs were synthesized using spirulina algae and amino acid l-arginine as a source of carbon and nitrogen via a bottom-up hydrothermal procedure at 180 °C. The particle size and morphology of N-CDs have been characterized using TEM and DLS. As illustrated in Fig. 1 a, b, the TEM and HR-TEM images revealed that the synthesized N-CDs were mostly monodispersed and the particle diameters were in the range of 2 to 8 nm with a mean size of 4.81 ± 0.24 nm. The mean hydrodynamic diameter of the N-CDs has been estimated at 3.4 to 8 nm based on the DLS measurement (Fig. 1c), suggesting that these N-CDs have an appropriate particle size distribution (PSD) and superior dispersal into water [36]. The Raman spectrum of the prepared N-CDs was presented in Fig. S1a; D-band at 1358 cm−1 and G-band at 1366 cm−1 were signature peaks for carbon which can be detected for N-CDs. They were attributed to the presence of sp3 and sp2 carbons, respectively. As has been proven, the intensity ratio of the d-band and G-band (ID/IG) denotes the carbon ratio of sp3/sp2. The computed ID/IG for N-CDs was 0.91, implying that they had an amorphous structure. The zeta potential for N-CDs measured at pH = 7 was shown to be −7.96 mV (Fig. S1b), which is caused by the presence of hydroxyls and carboxylic acids groups on its surfaces. According to Fig. 1d, the XRD pattern of the N-CDs demonstrated a wide diffraction peak at 2θ = 21° adapted to the (002) planes of the graphite structure, which designates the existence of graphitic lattice in the synthesized N-CDs structure. In comparison to the ordinary peak (002) at 26.5°, the shift of the as-synthesized N-CDs could be ascribed to the reduction of the orderly character of the sp2 layers because of the highly defective carbon structure on the N-CDs [37]. The chemical composition and functional groups on the N-CDs surface have been investigated by FT-IR spectroscopy. As can be seen from Fig. 1e, the absorption band at 3300 cm−1 and 3200 cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibrations of the OH bond and NH2 groups, respectively. Moreover, the absorption band at 2900 cm−1 could be ascribed to the CH bond. The absorption peak at 1600 and 1650 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibrations of the CO bond. There was a peak located at 1300–1400 cm−1 that was attributed to the bending vibrations of the CN bond. The band at (1000–1100 cm−1) aroused from the stretching vibrations of OC and the peaks at 2000 cm−1 were ascribed to the characteristic vibration of conjugate CC bonds, implying the appearance of the aromatic ring [38,39]. According to the FT-IR analysis above, the presence of different amino, carbonyl, and hydroxyl groups on the surface of the N-CDs can be achieved outstanding solubility in water and a high potential for sensing in solutions.

The XPS spectra were used to further testify the surface composition as FTIR and identified nitrogen doping in CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 a, N-CDs were composed of carbon (284 eV), nitrogen (400 eV), oxygen (532 eV), three elements, forming the present N-doped CDs. The full survey C1s spectrum of N-CDs in Fig. 2b mainly displayed three peaks, proving the presence of sp2 carbons (CC/CC, 282.8 eV), sp3 carbons (CN/CO, 284.4 eV), carbonyl, and carboxyl groups (CO/COH, 286.1 eV) [40]. As displayed in Fig. 2c, the spectrum of N1s could be deconvoluted into three surface content at 396.8 eV, 397.6 eV, and 401.3 eV, which should be attributed to the presence of CN, CNC, CNH2/ OCNH2 groups, respectively [35,41]. As illustrated in Fig. 2d, the two peaks at 529.8 eV and 532.1 eV on the high-resolution O1s XPS spectrum are ascribed to CO/COC and CO, respectively [42]. From the above findings, indicating that the functional groups as NH2/NH, COOH, and COH are integrated on the surface of N-CDs. Thus, N-CDs has the potential to be applied in biomedical applications.

The optical properties, including UV–vis absorption, fluorescence spectra, and quantum yield (QY) of the as-synthesized N-CDs were assessed. The absorption spectrum of N-CDs displays a peak at 288 nm (Fig. 3 a). The occurrence of a peak at 288 nm could be ascribed to the π- π * electron transition of the conjugated CC bond in the N-CDs surface [39]. Thus, depending on the HR-TEM image and UV–vis results, the green emission source of the as-synthesized N-CDs could be assigned to both surface defeat states and quantum size effects. The inset showed the N-CDs displayed intense green fluorescence when illuminated by a 450 nm UV lamp and generated the maximum emission at 530 nm when excited at 450 nm [39,43]. Therefore, its fluorescence emission demonstrated an excitation-dependent behavior on the excitation wavelength of 400 to 440 nm (Fig. 3b), which was similar to the familiar optics property of most of the N-CDs mentioned earlier [44]. The feature of excitation dependency was primarily ascribed to the surface defects produced using surface oxidation that can trap excitations and make variable energy gaps to induce a redshift in the N-CDs emission wavelength [45]. Contrary to the majority of N-CDs, while the excitation wavelength has been changed from 450 nm to 490 nm, the emission peak has been maintained at 530 nm, showing that the N-CDs had a fluorescence characteristic of independent excitation, which meant that the size of the synthesized N-CDs and the state of surface distribution could be highly uniform [46]. Additionally, The QY of the N-CDs could be found at 32 % using the Rhodamine 6G as a reference (Fig. S2).

Moreover, N-CDs stability was studied using the following experiment. The N-CDs were kept at room temperature for 1 month, and no evident change was observed at the peak emission (Fig. 4 a). Their fluorescence intensity remained the same when the concentration of NaCl reached 2 M (Fig. 4b). The fluorescence intensity remained unchanged following continuous irradiation of N-CDs under a UV lamp (450 nm) for 2 h (Fig. 4c). As depicted in Fig. 4d, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs was invariant in the pH value from 3 to 10, whereas the pH value was gradually reduced from 10 to 13. These findings implied that N-CDs had excellent stability and photostability that could be promising use for biological and sensing applications [37,38,47].

To explore the recognition capability of the probe detection system, the effect of various metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of obtained N-CDs were carried out at pH = 7 condition at 450 nm excitation, such as Co2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Na+, Mo6+, Mg2+, Li+, Ni+, Ag+, Pt4+, Pd2+, Pb2+, Rh3+, As+, Al3+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Se4+, K+, Cr3+. As shown in Fig. 5 , the N-CDs exhibited a significantly reduction in Fe3+ fluorescence among a variety of metal ions, portending that the N-CDs have specific selectivity and unique function toward Fe3+ ions. To verify the selectivity of N-CDs to Fe3+ ions, the competing experiment was conducted by adding Fe3+ ions to the N-CDs solution when other metal ions were present. As can be seen in Fig. 5, the lack of Fe3+ the competitive ions exhibited hardly change for the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs whereas observed a significant change after the addition of Fe3+ to the solution. According to the above findings, it demonstrated that the N-CDs had an excellent selectivity to the detection of Fe3+ even in complex environments, which could be possessed more significant opportunities for practical application [48–50].

As it can be seen in Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs can be quickly quenched in 120 s when the Fe3+ (0–50 µM) concentrations have been added to N-CDs solutions, demonstrating that the suitability probe to the selectivity of Fe3+ ions [51]. Fig. 6 b shows the relationship between the value of Fe3+ and the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs, which illustrates a good linear relationship in the scope of 0.05 to 1 µM with a correlation coefficient of 0.99. The limit of detection of Fe3+ as 14.8 nM is estimated on the basis of LOD = 3 σ / s ( σ is the standard deviation of blank N-CDs, and s is the intercept of the calculated standard curve). A comparison of the recently reported Fe3+ sensors is provided in Table 1 , and the N-CDs displayed significant selectivity and sensitivity.

Due to the practical application of a probe, its reversibility is extremely important. In order to verify the reversibility, the EDTA titration was conducted to verify the reversibility of N-CDs-Fe3+ species. The fluorescence intensity was quenched after the addition of Fe3+ ions to the N-CDs solution. When EDTA was added to the N-CDs-Fe3+ complex, the fluorescence intensity was restored completely. These results illustrate that Fe3+ in the N-CDs-Fe3+complex has been chelated by EDTA (Fig. 7 ). Remarkably, the quenching of the fluorescence intensity occurs again when Fe3+ is incorporated, ascertaining the reversible nature of the N-CDs probe for the detection of Fe3+ions. This process was repeated for at least three cycles to confirm that N-CDs was a reusable nanoprobe for detecting Fe3+ions. In short, we reckon that the observation and chemical structure analysis have connected the vital variation of PL intensity to the protonation and deprotonation of the amino groups on the surface of the as-prepared N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of amino acid using N-CDs-Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: To extend the potential application, the N-CDs-Fe3+ probe was investigated for detecting amino acids. Variations in the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs-Fe3+ probe owing to the incorporation of various amino acids were monitored to assess selectivity. As illustrated in Fig. 8 a, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs involving the Fe3+ ion shows a minimal emission peak at 530 nm under excitation at 450 nm. By adding l-alanine, the N-CDs-Fe3+ indicated a noticeable enhancement in fluorescence that may occur due to the formation of ternary complexes [31]. Meantime, the fluorescence peak (λ max) displayed a blueshift of 534 nm to 510 nm. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs-Fe3+ incremented by almost 2.2-fold after adding l-alanine to the solution compared to prior N-CDs-Fe3+. By adding l-histidine, the N-CDs-Fe3+ demonstrated a fluorescence increase. Compared with the fluorescence blue caused by N-CDs -Fe3+-l-histidine, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs-Fe3+ increased by nearly 2-fold after the addition of l-histidine that exhibited a red shift from 534 nm to 542 nm when excited at 450 nm. Nevertheless, amino acids such as l-alanine, l-histidine, l-glutamine, l-lysine, l-tryptophan, l-valine, l-tyrosine, l-arginine, l-cysteine, and l-glycine were studied in the presence of only N-CDs (Fig. 8b). However, none of these amino acids had apparent influences on fluorescence intensity. In accordance with the above findings, the N-CDs-Fe3+ act as a fluorescence turn-on capability for detecting l-histidine and l-alanine in an aqueous media.

Besides, As demonstrated in Fig. 9 and Fig. 10 , respectively, the fluorescence intensity at 530 nm and 542 nm increased linearly with l-alanine and l-histidine concentrations in the range of 0–100 µM and with a correlation coefficient of 0.996 and 0.994, respectively. The limit of detection for l-alanine and l-histidine were 52.8 nM and 69 nM, respectively. In accordance with the above findings, the N-CDs-Fe3+ act as a fluorescence turn-on capability for detecting l-histidine and l-alanine in an aqueous media.

Table 2 presents the linear range and detection limit of different sensing probes for the detection of l-alanine and l-histidine [30,31,56–58]. As seen, the linear range of this sensing probe was developed in comparison with the previously reported. Moreover, the detection limit achieved in the current work is either better than or at least comparable with most of the probes mentioned in Table 2.

To explore the fluorescence quenching mechanism for sensing Fe3+, fluorescence lifetime, UV–vis spectrum, zeta potential, FT-IR, energy level simulation, and cyclic voltammetry spectra were used to study the type and procedure of the quenching mechanism of a fluorescent [59]. The calculated fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+ were approximately 3.18 ns and 3.04 ns, respectively. No obvious change in the lifetime was observed after adding Fe3+ ions (Fig. 11 a); thus, the fluorescence quenching induced by Fe3+ might be ascribed to the static quenching, which further indicated the formation of the non-fluorescent complex between N-CDs and Fe3+ ions.

The UV–vis spectrum (Fig. S3) showed that a broad peak around 300 nm was appeared with the addition of Fe3+, revealing that the fluorescence quenching in the presence of Fe3+ was predominantly dominated by static quenching, which may result in the formation of the complex between Fe3+ and functional groups on the N-CDs surface. Furthermore, the zeta potential of N-CDs (Fig. S1b) was investigated, which showed a shift dramatically from −7.96 to −0.56 mV upon the incorporation of Fe3+, presumably due to the combination of Fe3+ with the hydroxyl or carboxyl groups and consumption of negative charge. To further confirmed that the quenching mechanism of fluorescent, the FT-IR spectra were employed to show the interaction between N-CDs and Fe3+ (Fig. S1c). By adding Fe3+, the peaks of the amino groups and carboxyl groups vanish to be displaced by a large wide peak [60]. Suggesting that the amino groups and the carboxyl groups all coordinate with Fe3+ ions to form the N-CDs- Fe3+complex. To further understanding the fluorescence quenching, the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) levels and the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the N-CDs were computed based on the following empirical formulas [15,61].
(1)
E HOMO
= -
e
E Rox
+ 4.4
(2)
E LUMO
= -
e
E Red
+ 4.4
(3)
E HOMO
=
E
LUMO
-
E g
where ERed and ERox were the reduction and oxidation potentials of N-CDs, respectively [7,59]. The ERed value of the N-CDs could be achieved at −0.44 eV through cyclic voltammetry curves (Fig. 11b). Therefore, the ELUMO was determined to be −3.95, which could be calculated using formula (1). Meanwhile, having the irreversible oxidation behavior caused that could not be obtained the EHUMO from formula (2). Thus, it was found to be −5.7 eV on the base of the formula (3); the Eg, which was the optical band gap resulting from the absorption edge in the absorption spectrum of N-CDs, was estimated to be 1.75 eV. In detail, the static quenching mechanism occurs through the formation complex in the ground state between the quencher and the CDs. Therefore, it has enabled that electrons transferred preferentially from the excited state of the LUMO orbital of the N-CDs to the unfilled d orbital of Fe3+, leading to the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs [62]. All of these results are in support of the formation complex between Fe3+ and the functional groups on the surface of N-CDs, which results in the quenching of fluorescence intensity Scheme 2 .

The feasibility of the quantitative proposed method for detecting Fe3+ in actual samples and the content of Fe3+ in tap and mineral water was evaluated. An absolute value of Fe3+ was spiked in the tap and mineral water, and the recoveries of Fe3+ were calculated to be 93–119 % and 96–103 %, respectively (Table 3 ). Therefore, these N-CDs can be employed as an effective nanoprobe for detecting Fe3+ in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility and cellular imaging capacity of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the reliability and the biosensing feasibility of N-CDs, the evaluation of cytotoxicity was conducted using an MTT assay with Hela cells. As depicted in Fig. 12 a, the growth of Hela cells in the presence of N-CDs solution and the mortality has been calculated. Mortality computation revealed that N-CDs was nontoxic in nature because the percentage of mortality was found to be less than 10 % even at a high concentration of N-CDs (1000 µg mL−1). Thus, the N-CDs appear to be promising agents for in vivo and in vitro; therefore, they could be easily substituted with conventional organic dyes having photobleaching features or semiconductor QDs biotoxicity in imaging [63].

To testify the feasibility of using N-CDs as a fluorescence probe for intracellular imaging and cell imaging of Fe3+ were studied by a microscopic image of Hela cells. Fig. 12b displays the treatment of Hela cells with the N-CDs (500 µg mL−1) incubated for 2 h. Obviously, intense green fluorescence emission was observed from the intracellular region for the Hela cells when it was excited at 450 nm, which distinctly indicates that the N-CDs can pass through cell membranes and penetrate cells. To further investigation, the application of N-CDs was conducted to semi-quantitively monitor the intracellular Fe3+ by using Hela cells with 50 µL Fe3+. As expected, the fluorescence has been shown extremely weak emission, indicating that N-CDs can be employed for cellular imaging and an effective probe for monitoring Fe3+ in the biosystem (Fig. 12b). These outcomes demonstrate that as-prepared N-CDs display biocompatibility with the cells, which makes them a potential candidate for biological fields.

To summarize, the N-CDs nanosensor was developed using spirulina algae and l-arginine via a bottom-up hydrothermal strategy with a quantum yield of 32 %. The N-CDs probe was found to be efficient for the sequential detection of Fe3+, l-alanine, and l-histidine. The limit of detection for Fe3+ by N-CDs was 14.8 nM. The electrons in the LUMO state of N-CDs would transfer to the half-filled 3d orbitals of Fe3+ ions, which further facilitated non-radiative electron/hole recombination and resulted in the consequently quenching of fluorescence. Afterward, the quenched fluorescence of the N-CDs-Fe3+complex could be restored by adding some amino acids, performing l-alanine and l-histidine detection. In comparison with the previous report, the N-CDs can detect l-alanine and l-histidine with a detection limit of 52.8 nM and 69 nM in the aqueous solution. Besides, these N-CDs have been applied for Fe3+ determination with high selectivity and excellent accuracy in real mineral water and tap water samples. The results reveal that this technique provides a new detection strategy for Fe3+ and certain amino acids with acceptable sensitivity and low cost, which could be exploited as a promising opportunity for practical application in bioimaging and the environment.

Elham Emami: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mohammad H. Mousazadeh: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are genuinely thankful to the Amirkabir University of Technology (Tehran, Iran), and the Iranian National Science Foundation (INSF-97016250) for the financial support of this research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2023.114536.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
